title: The Three Kings of Cologne
summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of U-M's 1999 digital encoding, keyed from the 1988 Kraus reprint of Carl Horstmann's 1886 EETS edition. Horstmann printed Cambridge University Library MS Ee.4.32 and British Library Royal MS 18 A X in parallel, supplied the damaged Cambridge opening from Cotton Titus A XXV, and added apparatus from a wider English manuscript and print tradition. U-M reorganized the parallel texts into separate divisions, retained the English variants, and omitted Horstmann's Latin text and marginal synopsis. The graph keeps the anonymous Middle English translation, digital encoding, reprint, edition, witnesses, and digital scan distinct.
review_notes: The witness graph is intentionally limited to the two directly transcribed texts, the separately evidenced Titus supply and Vespasian apparatus witnesses, and an aggregate description of Horstmann's remaining collation set; it is not a census of the more than twenty known witnesses.

U-M's SOURCEDESC uses the forms 'Ee.4.2' and 'Royal MS. 18.A.10'; its own table of contents and text headings, the edition, and the institutional descriptions establish the cataloged shelfmarks as Ee.4.32 and Royal MS 18 A X, which are used here.
No complete public manuscript facsimile was verified for Ee.4.32, Royal 18 A X, Cotton Titus A XXV, or Cotton Vespasian E XVI during this review.

entities.label: General Corpus XML artifact 3KCol
entities.description: The exact checked-in DLPSTEXTCLASS XML file in the pinned CME submodule.

entities.label: U-M Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse electronic text 3KCol
alternate_labels: University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative 3KCol
entities.description: A 1999 electronic encoding keyed from the 1988 Kraus reprint. Its declaration says that 'Chrisian' keyed the text to an assessed accuracy of one error per 20,000 characters; the identity and organizational type of that named keying contributor were not independently established. The encoding follows the print edition where possible but omits the Latin text and synoptic marginal gloss.

entities.label: Kraus Reprint of Horstmann's Three Kings of Cologne (1988)
entities.description: The immediate print source identified by the U-M header: a 1988 Millwood, New York reprint of Horstmann's 1886 EETS edition. No evidence of textual revision or fresh collation was found; the source describes it simply as a reprint.

bibliographic.citation: Carl Horstmann, ed., The Three Kings of Cologne: An Early English Translation of the Historia Trium Regum (Millwood, NY: Kraus Reprint, 1988), reprint of EETS OS 85 (1886).
bibliographic.title: The Three Kings of Cologne: An Early English Translation of the Historia Trium Regum
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 85

entities.label: Carl Horstmann's Three Kings of Cologne (EETS OS 85, 1886)
entities.description: Horstmann's parallel-text edition prints the Cambridge and Royal English versions on facing even and odd pages, English variant apparatus, readings from a Wynkyn de Worde print, readings from Cotton Vespasian E XVI, and a Latin text from page 206. The U-M encoding represents only a selection of this volume.

bibliographic.citation: Carl Horstmann, ed., The Three Kings of Cologne: An Early English Translation of the 'Historia Trium Regum' by John of Hildesheim, Edited from the MSS., Together with the Latin Text, EETS OS 85 (London: N. Trubner & Co., 1886), xxi, 312 pp.
bibliographic.title: The Three Kings of Cologne: An Early English Translation of the 'Historia Trium Regum'
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 85
bibliographic.publisher: N. Trubner & Co. for the Early English Text Society

entities.label: Google Books scan of Horstmann 1886 at Internet Archive
entities.description: A complete page-image scan of the 1886 EETS volume digitized by Google and distributed through Internet Archive under identifier threekingscolog00horsgoog. The reviewed PDF has 363 scan pages and includes Google's use notice before the historical volume.
entities.reproduction_format: Google Books page images distributed as PDF, OCR text, and derivative files by Internet Archive

entities.label: Frank Schaer's 1992–1993 study of The Three Kings of Cologne
entities.description: A three-volume doctoral thesis providing a diplomatic edition and a detailed study of the English manuscript tradition. It is supporting scholarship, not an upstream source of the 1886 Horstmann edition or the U-M encoding.

bibliographic.citation: Frank Schaer, The Three Kings of Cologne: A Diplomatic Edition of the Unabridged English Version of John of Hildesheim's Historia Trium Regum in Durham MS Hunter 15, with a Reconstruction of the Translator's Latin Text on Facing Pages Based on Corpus Christi College Cambridge MS 275, and a Study of the Manuscript Tradition (PhD thesis, University of Adelaide, submitted 1992; degree 1993), 3 vols.
bibliographic.title: The Three Kings of Cologne

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Ee.4.32, part 1
entities.description: The principal Cambridge witness used for Horstmann's even-page English text. The current IMEP handlist locates the standard prose Three Kings of Cologne at part 1, fols. 1r-23v, followed in the codex by a Middle English Prose Brut. Horstmann reports that the first page was unreadable from blackening and supplied its text from Cotton Titus A XXV. U-M's SOURCEDESC writes 'Ee.4.2,' but its own table of contents and text heading, Horstmann's edition, and the current handlist establish the shelfmark as Ee.4.32.

date_statements.label: manuscript production
date_statements.value: Fifteenth century; either half of the century has been proposed
date_statements.notes: The Faculty of English research note summarizes the Western Illuminated Manuscripts catalogue as dating the codex to the fifteenth century and citing either half as possible.

holding.notes: The exact item is described in the published IMEP Cambridge handlist. CUL states that most medieval manuscripts remain cataloged primarily in printed catalogues rather than ArchiveSearch.

physical_description.extent: The Three Kings of Cologne occupies separately foliated part 1, fols. 1r-23v
notes: The codex also contains the English Prose Brut; adjacent text is outside the cataloged work.

entities.label: Queen's University Belfast MS 59/215 microfilm of CUL Ee.4.32
entities.description: A microfilm surrogate held in QUB's Medieval Manuscripts on Microfilm collection. The finding aid identifies Ee.4.32 as MS 59/215 but does not state whether this particular reel covers the complete codex or selected parts.
entities.reproduction_format: Microfilm; exact completeness and generation not stated in the public finding aid

entities.label: British Library Royal MS 18 A X, item 15 (from fol. 87r)
alternate_labels: British Museum Royal MS 18 A X
entities.description: The first-group Royal recension printed by Horstmann on the odd pages of his parallel edition. The openings of chapters 1-32 spell the names Margareta Moningtown and Mawde Stranlea; Schaer's stemmatic analysis treats the Royal text as a deliberate secondary revision of the standard translation, not its original authorship signature.

date_statements.label: manuscript production
date_statements.value: First half of the fifteenth century

historical_designations: British Museum Royal MS 18 A X

physical_description.extent: Codex: i + 134 folios; The Three Kings of Cologne begins at fol. 87 and the following item begins at fol. 119v
notes: The Three Kings is item 15 in a twenty-item theological and devotional anthology.
An end at fol. 119r is inferred from the catalogue's statement that the following item begins at fol. 119v; the record does not give a separate explicit for item 15.

entities.label: British Library Cotton MS Titus A XXV, fols. 36r-71v
entities.description: A first-half-fifteenth-century witness of the standard prose translation. Horstmann supplied the unreadable opening page of the Cambridge text from this manuscript and cited it throughout the variant apparatus as T.

identifiers.value: Cotton MS Titus A XXV, ff. 36-71

date_statements.label: manuscript production
date_statements.value: First half of the fifteenth century

holding.shelfmark: Cotton MS Titus A XXV, ff. 36-71

physical_description.extent: The Three Kings of Cologne occupies fols. 36r-71v of a composite codex

entities.label: British Library Cotton MS Vespasian E XVI, fols. 38r-69v
entities.description: An imperfect mid-fifteenth-century witness closely related to the Royal recension. Horstmann printed its readings against the Royal text as a separate appendix; it ends at 'mete to,' corresponding to Royal page 153 line 24.

identifiers.value: Cotton MS Vespasian E XVI, ff. 38-85

holding.shelfmark: Cotton MS Vespasian E XVI, ff. 38-85
holding.notes: The exact component record contains several works; The Three Kings occupies only fols. 38r-69v.

physical_description.extent: The imperfect Three Kings of Cologne occupies fols. 38r-69v

entities.label: Horstmann's additional English collation witnesses
entities.description: An aggregate description of witnesses cited in the 1886 English apparatus beyond the separately modeled Cambridge, Royal, Titus, and Vespasian items: Bodleian Douce 301, British Library Harley 1704, the dated 1442 'Bedford' manuscript now BL Add MS 36983, Patrick Papers 43, Cambridge University Library Kk.1.3, and other readings at the scopes Horstmann states. It is not a claim that all had the same textual status or were used throughout every chapter.

entities.label: Wynkyn de Worde's first edition of The Three Kings of Cologne (c. 1499)
entities.description: An early printed version of the standard prose translation, conventionally dated 1499 although the edition itself omits the date. Horstmann states that it follows the Cambridge text or a copy but alters freely; his separate readings occupy pages 186-198.

date_statements.label: printing
date_statements.value: c. 1499; date omitted in the copy described by Horstmann

notes: A current item-level record for the surviving copy used by Horstmann was not verified in this review, so no present holding is asserted.

entities.label: John of Hildesheim, Historia trium regum
alternate_labels: Liber de gestis et translacionibus trium regum
entities.description: The widely transmitted Latin prose work about the Biblical Magi, their later lives, and the translation of their relics. It is the source work translated and abridged in 3KCol, not a physical exemplar identified as the translator's exact copy.

date_statements.label: composition
date_statements.notes: The terminus post quem is Florentius of Wevelinghoven's appointment as bishop of Munster in 1364; John died in 1375. A more precise date remains speculative.

agents.name: University of Michigan Library
alternate_names: University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative

agents.name: Queen's University Belfast Special Collections & Archives

scope.notes: The exact raw U-M delivery file was not separately downloaded, but the identifier, header, text, contents, and editorial declaration correspond to the public U-M item.

object.description: Title and contents, Horstmann's introduction, the Cambridge and Royal English texts, and English variant apparatus through print page 205

scope.notes: The printed Latin text on pp. 206-312 and the synoptic marginal gloss were omitted.
The source explicitly calls the 1988 issue a reprint; no correction, resetting, or new collation is asserted.

subject.description: Schaer's manuscript-tradition analysis and discussion of Horstmann

object.description: Horstmann's edition and its represented English manuscript tradition

scope.notes: This is a later scholarly description and critique, not a derivation edge.

subject.description: QUB microfilm MS 59/215; reel coverage is not specified in the finding aid

object.description: CUL Ee.4.32, possibly in full or in part

scope.notes: The collection-level description says MS 59 contains manuscript copies in full or in part, so this reel must not be called a complete facsimile without inspection or confirmation.

subject.description: Cambridge English text on the even pages of the parallel edition

scope.notes: The manuscript's blackened opening was not legible enough to serve as the source for print page 2; that opening was supplied from Cotton Titus A XXV. Horstmann also inserted readings from other witnesses.

subject.description: Royal English text on the odd pages of the parallel edition

locators: begins fol. 87r; inferred through fol. 119r because the next item begins fol. 119v

subject.description: The opening of the Cambridge text and bracketed or noted readings

object.description: Corresponding readings in Cotton Titus A XXV

scope.notes: The first page of Cambridge Ee.4.32 was largely unreadable; Horstmann's opening note and U-M declaration identify Titus as the supply source.

subject.description: The Cambridge text, introduction, editorial notes, and English variant apparatus

object.description: The witnesses and scopes listed by Horstmann in his apparatus heading

scope.notes: Horstmann cites Titus A XXV, Douce 301, Harley 1704, Bedford/Add MS 36983, Patrick Papers 43, CUL Kk.1.3, and the early print at different scopes. The aggregate avoids implying uniform whole-text collation or identical textual status.

subject.description: Readings to the Royal manuscript text

object.description: The imperfect Three Kings of Cologne

subject.description: Readings in Wynkyn de Worde's first edition

object.description: The corresponding early printed standard prose version

subject.description: The standard Middle English prose translation represented by the Cambridge and Royal texts

object.description: John of Hildesheim's Latin source work

scope.notes: This work-level translation relation does not assert direct dependence on a particular surviving Latin manuscript.

rights.component: Horstmann's 1886 edition, transcription, introduction, apparatus, and the underlying medieval texts
rights.basis: The inspected volume was published in London in 1886; U-M currently presents this exact item as public domain and assigns No Copyright-United States, while Internet Archive marks the scan NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT.

rights.component: Any new 1988 cover, prefatory, editorial, or other reprint-specific contributions beyond the unchanged 1886 edition
rights.jurisdiction: United States and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: The underlying 1886 edition is public domain in the United States, but no copy of the 1988 reprint or item-specific notice was inspected to determine whether it added protectable modern matter.

rights.component: Materials presented in U-M CME item 3KCol
rights.basis: U-M currently states that the materials are in the public domain and assigns No Copyright-United States.
notes: NoC-US is jurisdiction-specific and is not a license or a worldwide public-domain determination.
The 1999 XML contains a narrower redistribution restriction whose relationship to the current statement is unresolved.

rights.component: The electronic transcription and markup as distributed with the 1999 availability statement
rights.basis: The embedded notice permits personal, research, and teaching copies with the notice retained and permits linking, but expressly forbids redistribution by commercial or not-for-profit content providers.
notes: This records an express historical provider condition separately from copyright status; it does not decide whether U-M's current NoC-US statement supersedes it.

rights.jurisdiction: United States and inherited provider terms
rights.basis: U-M currently labels the item public domain in the United States and the historical edition is public domain, but the checked-in XML still embeds U-M's 1999 redistribution restriction. The General Corpus and CME repository licenses do not by themselves resolve inherited provider terms for this exact file.

rights.component: The Google-generated PDF, page images, OCR derivatives, and added Google use notice
rights.basis: The source edition is public domain in the United States and Internet Archive marks the item NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT, while the PDF includes Google's requested non-commercial-use, attribution, and anti-automation conditions.

rights.component: Schaer's thesis text and University of Adelaide PDF files
rights.jurisdiction: Australia and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: The University of Adelaide makes the thesis publicly downloadable under its open-access policy but states that copyright remains with the author and identifies no open reuse license.
notes: Public research access and citation do not by themselves authorize republication of the thesis or its incorporated manuscript images.

rights.component: Selected modern photographs of Ee.4.32 on the Manuscripts Lab page
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: The selected-image research note does not provide an item-level reuse license, and CUL's imaging service distinguishes personal research scans from publication-quality images and permission.

rights.component: QUB's microfilm surrogate and any supplied digital reproduction
rights.basis: QUB offers consultation and considers film-media digitisation and reproduction requests individually; the public finding aid provides no item-specific reuse license.
notes: Confirm coverage, cost, delivery format, and redistribution or publication terms in writing before treating a supplied file as a complete reusable facsimile.

rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: The exact catalogue record exposes no digitized-content link and gives no image-specific reuse grant.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Distinct Cambridge and Royal recensions of the standard Middle English prose translation
editorial_practices.representation: Cambridge on even pages and Royal on facing odd pages, followed by a separate variant apparatus
editorial_practices.description: Horstmann did not silently conflate the two principal recensions. His edition lets their readings be compared in parallel while adding notes and variants from other witnesses.
editorial_practices.scope: English texts on pp. 2-158 and English apparatus on pp. 159-205

editorial_practices.source_feature: A blackened Cambridge opening, omissions, corrupt readings, and textual variation across manuscripts and early print
editorial_practices.representation: Supplied Cambridge opening from Titus A XXV; brackets, notes, corrections, and back-matter variants identify other sources
editorial_practices.description: The Cambridge printed text is an edited witness representation rather than a purely diplomatic transcription. Notes may identify supplied text, readings from other manuscripts, or proposed corrections.
editorial_practices.scope: Cambridge text, notes, and variant apparatus

editorial_practices.source_feature: Horstmann's complete volume, including English texts and apparatus, a synoptic marginal gloss, and Latin text
editorial_practices.representation: English title matter, introduction, witness texts, and variants retained; synoptic marginal gloss and Latin text omitted
editorial_practices.description: The U-M item is not a complete-content transcription or facsimile of the 1988 reprint. It ends with the English apparatus at print page 205 even though the source volume continues with Latin through page 312.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Facing Cambridge and Royal texts in print
editorial_practices.representation: Separate DIV1 nodes 3KCol:3 and 3KCol:4, followed by apparatus nodes 3KCol:5-3KCol:7
editorial_practices.description: U-M serializes the complete Cambridge text before the complete Royal text rather than reproducing the print's page-by-page parallel arrangement. The variant readings remain back matter.
editorial_practices.scope: BODY and BACK of the electronic text

editorial_practices.source_feature: Cambridge headings partly supplied by Horstmann; Royal print text lacks displayed chapter headings
editorial_practices.representation: Cambridge headings retained, with Horstmann supplies in brackets; all Royal electronic headings supplied by U-M, and five additional unheaded section breaks retained
editorial_practices.description: The XML's 40 Royal section nodes are electronic navigation and comparison structure, not a direct record of manuscript rubrication.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Words split by manuscript folio or printed page boundaries
editorial_practices.representation: Page-broken words are joined and placed before PB; 'Chal/deys' is joined as 'Chaldeys' before a milestone, while the printed hyphen in 'to-gedir' is retained
editorial_practices.description: These localized interventions facilitate searching while retaining milestone placement; they should not be read as photographs of manuscript word division.
editorial_practices.scope: Electronic word division and page/folio milestones

evidence.locator: FILEDESC, SOURCEDESC, ENCODINGDESC, FRONT, BODY, and BACK
evidence.summary: The exact file identifies U-M's 1999 encoding and 3KCol ID, the 1988 Kraus reprint and 1886 edition, Cambridge and Royal source texts, keying accuracy, notes and variant practices, supplied headings, word-division changes, omitted Latin and marginal synopsis, and the embedded availability restriction. Its SOURCEDESC shelfmark forms conflict with the more precise forms in the same encoding's contents and text headings.

evidence.citation: Kyvero Press, CME and General-Corpus repositories on GitHub.
evidence.locator: Repository identity, CME submodule pin, and license statements
evidence.summary: Confirms the public Kyvero CME source repository and General Corpus parent repository; the parent currently pins CME at e1d77c8. Repository-level license statements do not expressly resolve the inherited 1999 U-M restriction embedded in this exact XML file.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item 3KCol.
evidence.locator: Item information, rights, actions, and contents
evidence.summary: Identifies Horstmann, the 1988 Kraus publication, public full-text access, current public-domain and No Copyright-United States statements, and a table of contents ending with the English Vespasian variants rather than the printed Latin text.

evidence.citation: Carl Horstmann, ed., The Three Kings of Cologne, EETS OS 85 (London: N. Trubner, 1886), Google Books scan at Internet Archive, threekingscolog00horsgoog.
evidence.locator: Title page; introduction pp. v-xxi; English texts pp. 2-158; apparatus pp. 159-205; Latin text pp. 206-312; Internet Archive metadata and PDF front notice
evidence.summary: The inspected scan confirms title-page publication and responsibility, manuscript descriptions and editorial argument, the parallel English layout, exact apparatus headings and sigla, the separate Wynkyn and Vespasian sections, the Latin continuation, scan provenance, access formats, and provider notices.

evidence.citation: Frank Schaer, The Three Kings of Cologne: A Diplomatic Edition of the Unabridged English Version ... and a Study of the Manuscript Tradition, PhD thesis, University of Adelaide, submitted October 1992 (degree 1993).
evidence.summary: Provides the most detailed reviewed analysis of the standard translation and its more than twenty witnesses, distinguishes other Middle English versions, establishes the conventional c. 1400 date and hard bounds, analyzes the Cambridge, Royal, Titus, Vespasian, and printed branches, and qualifies Horstmann's editorial reconstruction. The repository exposes front matter and all three thesis volumes as four PDF files and states that copyright remains with the author.

evidence.citation: Margaret Connolly, 'Ee.4.32,' Index of Middle English Prose, Handlist XIX: Manuscripts in the University Library, Cambridge (Boydell & Brewer, 2009), pp. 117-118.
evidence.summary: Gives the exact shelfmark and modern part foliation, incipit, explicit, Latin explicit rubric, identification as the standard prose version, Horstmann edition reference, and adjacent Brut content.

evidence.citation: Thai-Catherine Matthews, 'MS Ee.4.32 and the Case of the Disappearing Pope,' The Manuscripts Lab, Faculty of English, University of Cambridge, 23 February 2022.
evidence.locator: Opening description and selected manuscript images
evidence.summary: Describes Ee.4.32 as a membrane manuscript containing The Three Kings of Cologne and the English Prose Brut, summarizes a fifteenth-century dating with either half possible, and publishes selected images of Brut fols. 82r, 85r, 88v, and 90v rather than the Three Kings portion or a complete facsimile.

evidence.citation: Cambridge University Library, Catalogues, Special Collections.
evidence.summary: CUL explains that records for most medieval manuscripts are still available primarily in printed catalogues rather than ArchiveSearch, supporting use of the exact published IMEP handlist and an institutional enquiry route instead of inventing an online item record.

evidence.citation: Cambridge University Library, Visiting Special Collections.
evidence.locator: Reader registration, ordering material, and contact details
evidence.summary: Explains registration, advance requests by classmark, onsite consultation, fees for some non-UK and public reader cards, and the Special Collections contact route.

evidence.citation: Cambridge University Library, Request a Scan from Special Collections.
evidence.locator: Eligibility, image quality, publication-quality route, copyright restrictions, and conditions of supply
evidence.summary: Distinguishes research-only scans from publication images, limits the free Scan and Deliver service to eligible Cambridge users and reasonable orders, subjects delivery to conservation and copyright review, and prohibits redistribution of research scans.

evidence.citation: Queen's University Belfast Special Collections & Archives, MS 59 Medieval Manuscripts on Microfilm Collection finding aid.
evidence.locator: Collection description and Cambridge entry for MS Ee.4.32, QUB reference MS 59/215
evidence.summary: Confirms that QUB holds a microfilm surrogate of Cambridge University Library MS Ee.4.32 as MS 59/215. The collection contains manuscript copies in full or in part and the item row does not specify this reel's coverage. QUB states that microfilms can be consulted with advance notice and through its Virtual Reading Room.

evidence.citation: Queen's University Belfast Special Collections & Archives, Reprographics.
evidence.locator: Personal-research reproductions, publication images, film-media digitisation, fees, and contact details
evidence.summary: Explains that reproduction is discretionary and subject to rights and condition review, provides research and publication request routes, and says film-media digitisation requests are considered individually by quotation.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Royal MS 18 A X, record 040-002107426.
evidence.locator: Item 15 at fol. 87; date, physical description, access, and Digitised Content field
evidence.summary: Dates the vellum codex to the first half of the fifteenth century, locates Horstmann's Three Kings source at fol. 87, describes the acrostic recension and 134-folio codex, gives physical consultation rules, and exposes no digitized-content link.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Cotton MS Titus A XXV, ff. 36-71, record 041-001607013.
evidence.locator: Exact component record, date, language, access, and Digitised Content field
evidence.summary: Identifies the Middle English Historia trium regum component at fols. 36-71, dates it to the first half of the fifteenth century, records England as origin, gives physical consultation rules, and exposes no digitized-content link.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Cotton MS Vespasian E XVI, ff. 38-85, record 041-001103442, with parent record 040-001103439.
evidence.locator: Exact component contents and date; parent physical description, access, and Digitised Content field
evidence.summary: Locates the imperfect Three Kings at fols. 38r-69v, dates the component to the mid fifteenth century, records Middle English, describes the parchment parent codex and physical consultation route, and exposes no digitized-content link.

Defines NoC-US as the provider's belief that the item is public domain under U.S. law, expressly leaves other countries unresolved, and states that the notice is not a license and may coexist with contract restrictions.

open_questions.question: Does U-M's current No Copyright-United States statement supersede or otherwise modify the 1999 XML restriction against redistribution by commercial or not-for-profit content providers?
next_steps: Request a written interpretation from U-M Library before redistributing the XML or a close derivative as a content provider.
Keep copyright status, repository licenses, and inherited provider terms separate in downstream publication decisions.

open_questions.question: Is a complete institutional digital surrogate or more precise current item-level catalog record available for Cambridge University Library MS Ee.4.32?
next_steps: Ask CUL Rare Books and Early Manuscripts staff whether an unindexed digital surrogate or fuller internal record exists.
Ask QUB whether MS 59/215 covers the complete codex and whether a complete digital transfer can be supplied through the Virtual Reading Room or reprographics service.
Request fols. 1r-23v through the appropriate imaging route and obtain written reuse terms before publication.
Consult the printed Western Illuminated Manuscripts catalogue directly to record the evidence behind its two alternative half-century datings.

open_questions.question: Do complete digital images of Royal MS 18 A X, Cotton Titus A XXV, or Cotton Vespasian E XVI exist behind unavailable legacy services or by mediated request, and what terms govern reuse?
next_steps: Ask British Library Western Manuscripts Reference Services about existing surrogates for the exact folio ranges.
Request written image-supply and publication terms rather than inferring permission from the public catalog records.

open_questions.question: Which exact words and passages in Horstmann's Cambridge and Royal printed texts derive from each auxiliary witness rather than the named principal manuscript?
next_steps: Map every bracket, note, and apparatus siglum to the corresponding witness and compare against page images.
Use Schaer's stemmatic apparatus to distinguish Horstmann's supplies, emendations, and inherited witness readings.

open_questions.question: Who or what was 'Chrisian,' the keying contributor named in the 1999 U-M encoding declaration, and what surviving production or quality-control records document the electronic text?
next_steps: Ask U-M whether project files identify the vendor or person and preserve keying, correction, or QA logs.
Do not silently normalize the name to 'Christian' without documentary support.
